text.title: Gateway trade-offs — compliance, features, observability
text.summary: What you give up when you go through a gateway, and the rules for keeping the cost win without losing the vendor-feature win.

section.title: Compliance and data residency
section.p: The gateway becomes a third-party data processor. For regulated workloads (HIPAA, GDPR, SOC 2 Type II, defense), the gateway must either be self-hosted (LiteLLM proxy, Kong AI Gateway) inside the same compliance boundary as the application, or excluded entirely.

rule.statement: If the workload is subject to data-residency or compliance constraints, either self-host the gateway inside the same boundary or call the provider SDK directly and implement the fallback chain in application code.
rule.rationale: Hosted gateways such as OpenRouter route data through their own infrastructure; this can break data-residency contracts and add a third-party processor to the audit scope.

section.reference: Kong AI Gateway — self-hosted PII sanitization in front of LLMs.

section.title: Vendor-feature parity
section.p: Gateways trail vendor SDKs on cutting-edge features. Anthropic prompt caching, OpenAI Batch API, Gemini long-context, and per-vendor fine-tunes typically take weeks to land in any gateway after release.

rule.statement: For any workload that depends on a vendor-specific feature (prompt caching, batch API, fine-tunes), call the vendor SDK directly and implement the fallback chain at the application layer; do not route those calls through a gateway.
rule.rationale: The cost win from prompt caching (~90% on cached input) is far larger than the cost win from gateway routing for those workloads; routing through the gateway typically loses the cache.

example.note: Direct call preserves prompt caching; fallback chain still runs in app code on real failures.

antipattern.description: Routing every call through the gateway "for consistency" while paying for prompt-caching workloads.
antipattern.reason: Loses the cache discount on the workloads that benefit from it the most; the gateway saving is dwarfed by the cache loss.

section.title: Observability and billing
section.p: The gateway is a natural choke-point for telemetry: every call gets a trace, a cost record, and a per-model success-rate metric. Bill only successful runs (Zero Completion Insurance) so failed fallbacks do not show up on the invoice.

rule.statement: Emit OTel GenAI spans for every gateway call with attributes for primary_model, used_model, fallback_count, and cost_usd; alert when fallback_count rises above a baseline.
rule.rationale: Rising fallback rate is the leading indicator of vendor incidents and quota exhaustion; without per-call telemetry the first signal is angry users.
